coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent nanocellulosic hydrogels based on graphene quantum dots for sensing laccase
coredata.dc:creator: Ruiz-Palomero, Celia
Benítez-Martínez, Sandra
Soriano, M. Laura
Valcárcel, Miguel
coredata.dc:description: A novel low-cost fluorimetric platform based on sulfur, nitrogen-codoped graphene quantum dots immersed into nanocellulosic hydrogels is designed and applied in detecting the laccase enzyme. Although most of methods for detecting laccase are based on their catalytic activity, which is strongly dependent on environmental parameters, we report a sensitive and selective method based on the fluorescence response of hydrogels containing graphene quantum dots (GQDs) acting as luminophore towards laccase. The easily-prepared gel matrix not only improves the fluorescence signal of GQDs by avoiding their self-quenching but also stabilizes their fluorescence signal and improves their sensitivity towards laccase. Noncovalent interactions between the sensor and the analyte are believed to be causing this significant quenching without peak-shifts of GQD fluorescence via energy transfer. The selective extraction of laccase was proved in different shampoos as complex matrices achieving a detection limit of 0.048 U mL−1 and recoveries of 86.2–94.1%. As the unusual properties of nanocellulose and GQDs, the fluorescent sensor is simple, eco-friendly and cost-efficient. This straightforward strategy is able to detect and stabilize laccase, being an added-value for storage and recycling enzymes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent hydrogel
Nanocellulose
S,N-codoped graphene quantum dots
Enzyme
Laccase
Sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of sulfur and nitrogen codoped graphene quantum dots
Formation of the sensor hybrid hydrogel
Preparation and characterization of nanomaterials and fluorescent hydrogel
Behavior of the fluorescent hydrogel towards laccase enzyme
Determination of laccase in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HR-TEM (inset image) images of S,N-GQDs (a). IR spectra of the hydrogel and the individual nanoparticles (b); the blue line (1) represents the IR curve of the S,N-GQDs/NC hydrogel, the black line (2) shows the IR spectrum of the S,N-GQDs and the red line the IR curve of NC (3). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Synthetic approaches used to form reversible sustainable fluorescent gels. (a) Route for preparing NC hydrogels and (b) fluorescent hydrogels via S,N-GQDs non covalently immobilized in a hydrogel formed by TEMPO-oxidized NC.
Photographs under UV irradiation of the hydrogels of NC in absence and presence of S,N-GQDs (a) and the SEM image of the fluorescent GQDs/NC hydrogels (b).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the GQDs/NC hydrogels in absence and presence of different concentrations of the enzyme, and (b) the calibration graph of the developed sensing methodology. The equation was y = 0.2506x+1.2079 (R2 = 0.997).

head.ce:title: Fluorescent nanocellulosic hydrogels based on graphene quantum dots for sensing laccase

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Institute of Fine Chemistry and Nanochemistry, Marie Curie Building, Campus de Rabanales, University of Córdoba, E-14071 Córdoba, Spain

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Fine Chemistry and Nanochemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel low-cost fluorimetric platform based on sulfur, nitrogen-codoped graphene quantum dots immersed into nanocellulosic hydrogels is designed and applied in detecting the laccase enzyme. Although most of methods for detecting laccase are based on their catalytic activity, which is strongly dependent on environmental parameters, we report a sensitive and selective method based on the fluorescence response of hydrogels containing graphene quantum dots (GQDs) acting as luminophore towards laccase. The easily-prepared gel matrix not only improves the fluorescence signal of GQDs by avoiding their self-quenching but also stabilizes their fluorescence signal and improves their sensitivity towards laccase. Noncovalent interactions between the sensor and the analyte are believed to be causing this significant quenching without peak-shifts of GQD fluorescence via energy transfer. The selective extraction of laccase was proved in different shampoos as complex matrices achieving a detection limit of 0.048 U mL−1 and recoveries of 86.2–94.1%. As the unusual properties of nanocellulose and GQDs, the fluorescent sensor is simple, eco-friendly and cost-efficient. This straightforward strategy is able to detect and stabilize laccase, being an added-value for storage and recycling enzymes.

ce:section.ce:para: Development of effective nanotools for enzyme detection is an attractive long term analytical topic [1,2]. Compared to solid and liquid nanotechnological platforms, the self-assembly of sensitive nanoparticles (NPs) in different media as hydrogels is an interesting approach to create more sophisticated stable sensing platforms [3–6], which avoids aggregation of the sensitized nanoparticles and then erroneous measurements due to the fluorescence self-quenching and the diffusion problem in the solid film state.

Literature of this novel subject was found to be expanded by the large number of applications dealing with synthetic and natural gels [7]. About the technologies adopted for hydrogel [8] production, natural polymers were gradually holding the top positions by virtue of its abundance, biodegradability, easy extraction and modification design. Nanocellulose (NC) is getting a tremendous attention and has been postulated as one of the promising nanomaterial [9] in the future to revolutionize electronics [10], pharmaceutical engineering [11], environmental and health protection [12,13] among others, by virtue of its abundance and biodegradable character, mass production at low cost from renewable natural resources, and their fascinating properties.

A new concept to create new functional materials by combining hydrogels and nanoparticles was recently proposed [14]. Herein we are interested in producing innovative hydrogels containing two types of biocompatible nanomaterials, one acting as the gelator, and the other as the sensitizer. Renewable and sustainable NC nanofibers comforting the gel matrix are easily stabilized by the electrostatic charges whereas the unique optical properties of fluorescent nanodots [15] resulted in an excellent combination of properties in a synergistic manner. Amongst fluorescent nanodots, carbonaceous dots seem to be eligible candidates for analytical applications by virtue of their non-toxic nature if compared to semiconductor quantum dots. As such, the incorporation of zero-dimensional GQDs because of their fascinating properties (optical transparency, high resistant and electron mobility amongst others) leads to a new generation of advanced sensing probes as alternative to the conventional ones thanks to both quantum confinement and edge effects of such nanosheets [16]. It is expected that the advantages of water-soluble GQDs supported in such gel matrix rely on the removal of self-quenching of their fluorescence, as well as the possibility of easily gelation and enhancement of its intrinsic fluorescence features.

Laccase belongs to the multicopper oxidase family of enzymes. The great interest of laccase lies in their catalytic activity towards a wide variety of substances in presence of molecular oxygen as electron acceptor [17]. In fact, laccase is involved in the monoelectronic oxidation of several aromatic compounds and aliphatic amines; thus, many commercial applications are described such as improving whiteness in a conventional bleaching of fabrics [18], tooth whitening products [19], determination of aromatic compounds, and oxidation of dyes in beer, must and wines and in waste water purification. Recent research focuses on the incorporation of laccase in the compositions of hair dye [20,21] decolouration and coloration [22] products. For these reasons, monitoring enzyme laccase in commercial products is of great interest. Notably, most studies involving the determination of laccase deal with their catalytic activity monitoring. However, there are many inhibitors of the Trametes versicolor enzyme such as organic and inorganic substrates (phenol derivatives, aromatic amines, ascorbate and metal ions) but also inducers of the laccase activity by the addition of fallic and ferulyc acids, xylidine, syringaldazine, veratryl alcohol amongst others. Due to the simplicity, low cost, and sensitivity of fluorimetric techniques, only few authors reported the determination of laccase Trametes versicolor using azidocoumarin as a selective fluorescence sensor [23]. In this regard, methods for monitoring the laccase enzyme without evaluating their enzymatic activity are needed.

To address this, the combination of such challenging nanomaterials, biodegradable NC for their fascinating rheology and stabilizing properties and non-toxic sulfur and nitrogen codoped graphene quantum dots (S,N-GQDs) for their sensitive and sensible fluorescence features, constituted a novel platform for laccase determination without the evaluation of its enzymatic activity. Herein, we developed a cost-effective fluorimetric method to extract and determine laccase from a complex matrix, with the advantages of storage and posterior reusability of the enzyme in such hydrogel for further applications.

Laccase from Trametes versicolor (0.6–1.07 U mg−1 protein), acetylcholinesterase from Electrophorus electricus (200–1000 U mg−1 protein), salicylic acid (≥99%), citric acid monohydrate (99–102%) and thiourea (99%), Avicel PH-101 cellulose microcrystalline (50 μm of particle size), 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine-1-oxyl radical (TEMPO, >98%), sodium hypochlorite solution (10–15%), sodium bromide (>99%), sodium hydroxide pellets (>97%), magnesium sulfate anhydrous (≥99.99), cobalt(II) nitrate hexahydrate (99.999%), calcium chloride anhydrous (99.99%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Madrid, Spain); ethanol (absolute) was purchased from Scharlab (Barcelona, Spain). All reagents were used as used without further purification. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ) was obtained through a Milli-Q purification system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA) and used for the preparation of the hydrogels, enzyme stock solutions and other standards.
Dialysis tubings with a cut-off (MWCO) of 3.5 kDa were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Pur-A-Lazer™ Mega 3500). Shampoos were purchased from a local supermarket.

Fluorescence measurements and absorption spectrum were performed on a PTI QuantaMaster™ Spectrofluorometer (Photon Technology International) equipped with a 75 W xenon short arc lamp and halogen light source coupled to a model 814 PMT detection system. The system control, data acquisition and data analysis were performed by FeliX32 software. Infrared (IR) spectra were performed in a Shimadzu IRPRESTIGE-21 spectrometer equipped with an attenuated total reflectance (ATR) device.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were obtained at 300 kV using a Tecnai F30 FEG TEM equipped with a windowless X-MAX Si drift detector. Hydrogel was imaged using scanning electron microscope (SEM) JEOL JSM 6300 using a beam setting of 30 kV, after drying under vacuum and coating with a 3-nm chromium layer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was performed on a PHOBIOS 150 MCD 5700 spectrometer with non-monochromatic Al Kα radiation and a multichannel detector.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of sulfur and nitrogen codoped graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The codoped GQDs were synthesized as previously described [24] with slight modifications. In short, a solution containing citric acid (0.42 g, 2.19 mmol) and thiourea (0.46 g, 6.04 mmol) 10 mL of ultrapure water were introduced into a Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave reactor, which was sealed and heated for 3 h at 160 °C. After cooling at room temperature, basification of the aqueous solution containing the S,N-GQDs until pH 6 (using 1 M NaOH) and then purification by dialysis (3.5 kDa cut-off) for 6 h was performed. Purified fluorescent dot solution at a concentration of 8 mg mL−1 was stored at 4 °C until use. Batch to batch of the synthesis, calculated in fluorescence intensity, showed a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 1.2%.

The preparation of oxidized NC was performed as previously reported [25]. Briefly, cellulose microcrystalline (5 g) and NaBr (12.5 mg, 1.21 mmol) were mixed with TEMPO free radical (145 mg, 0.08 mmol) in aqueous solution at room temperature. Then, NaOCl (40 mL, 648.1 mmol) were added dropwise onto the mixture while keeping the pH of the resulting solution at 10. After the addition, the mixture is stirred at room temperature until no more changes in the pH were observed. Then, ethanol was added and the suspension filtered and washed several times with methanol and water, and finally, dried under vacuum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation of the sensor hybrid hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: For the hydrogel formation, 45 mg of NC, 250 μL of diluted S,N-GQDs (2.7 mg mL−1) and 250 μL of ultrapure water were placed into a sealed 2 mL quartz vial. The mixture was stirred via vortex for 5 s, sonicated for 10 s in an ultrasonic bath and heated for 30 s with a heat gun. Transparent hydrogels were formed in few minutes on cooling to ambient temperature. Their formation was monitored by inversion test. Batch to batch reproducibility of the S,N-GQDs/NC hydrogel, in terms of fluorescence intensity, resulted to have a RSD of 1.3%.

Fluorimetric analysis was directly performed at room temperature in the quartz vial in which the gelation was performed, setting an excitation wavelength at 376 nm and the emission range between 390 and 600 nm (maximum peak found at 450 nm). Both slit widths at excitation and emission were set at 2 nm unless otherwise indicated. The determination of the enzyme was achieved by monitoring the fluorescence of S,N-GQDs after 10 min of interaction enzyme-GQD in aqueous solution prior to the gelation.

In a typical sensing experiment, 250 μL of S,N-GQDs and 250 μL of enzyme aqueous solutions at different concentrations were put in contact into a quartz vials for 10 min to ensure the interaction between the fluorescence dots and the target analyte. Then, 45 mg of NC were added to the solution and the hydrogel were formed as previously described, being their fluorescence monitored.

Color protect shampoo samples were fortified with laccase aqueous solution at two different concentration levels, 0.15 and 5 U mL−1 as follows: 1 mL of the enzyme aqueous solution were added to 0.2 g of shampoo and mixed via vortex for 1 min until homogenization and lead to stand overnight at 4 °C.

With the purpose of extracting the enzyme from the shampoo matrix, a liquid-liquid extraction (LLE) was performed at first. To this end, 1 mL of ethyl acetate and 1 mL of hexane was added to the sample, mixed and decanted until the separation of different phases occur. The nonpolar phase, containing the organic ingredients was discarded and the aqueous phase was filtered through a non-commercial solid phase extraction (SPE) 3-mL cartridges packed containing 50 mg polyvinylpolypyrrolidone to remove possible interferences from the matrix [26]. The sorbent was previously conditioning for the improvement of their retention capacity; therefore, the packed polyvinylpolypyrrolidone was rinsed with 250 μL of methanol followed by 800 μL of ultrapure water. Experimental tests corroborated the lack of retention of the enzyme into the SPE sorbent. In this case, the sensing hydrogel was formed as previously described. Namely, 250 μL of S,N-GQDs and 250 μL of the extractant were kept interacting for 10 min and, afterwards, 45 mg of NC were added into the quartz vial for gelation. Fluorescence measurements were made and the obtained quenching data corroborated that laccase was not retained in the sorbent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of nanomaterials and fluorescent hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: In recent works, assemblies of fluorescent systems into hydrogels were reported to significantly enhance not only its intrinsic fluorescence but also the sensitivity towards certain targets [4,5,27], especially those gels containing fluorescent nanodots [15].
Hence, the introduction of S,N-GQDs [28] into NC hydrogels lead to the formation of very stable fluorescent 3D systems with interesting optical properties.

Circular flat sheets of the S,N-GQDs used in this work shows an average diameter of 3.5 ± 0.5 nm, as indicated by HR-TEM (Fig. 1 a). The blue excitation-independent emission of S,N-GQDs could be attributed to the ordered sp2 clusters within the sp3 C-O matrix, which is further bolstered by the results of IR and XPS analyses. The highest fluorescence intensity appears at 440 nm when excited at 376 nm, and the quantum yield (Φ) is calculated to be Φ = 30% using quinine sulfate as reference. Evaluation of the fluorescence signal over the S,N-GQDs concentration gave us information about the self-quenching of fluorescence at concentrations higher than 2 mg mL−1. Thus, no aggregation was observed at concentrations ranging 0.8–2.0 mg mL−1, being the highest fluorescence intensity resulted to be in this later case, as depicted in Fig. S1.

The typical adsorption band attributed to the transition from the highest occupied molecular is quite red-shifted, appearing at 390 nm (Fig. S2), as an indication of the alteration of the chemical composition of nanolayers by nitrogen and sulfur atoms.

Fig. 1b illustrates the IR spectra of the resulting hydrogel and the individual nanomaterials, S,N-GQDs and NC. The IR spectrum of S,N-GQDs shows a broad absorption band at around 3170 cm−1 attributed to the vibrations mode of O–H and N-H functional groups at the edges. The peaks at 1705 cm−1 and 1666 cm−1 could be assigned to the C=O stretching mode of amide and carboxyl groups, respectively, whereas the peaks at 1566 cm−1 and 1390 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of C=C and C–N bonds, respectively. The stretching vibration of the -C=S appears at 1086 cm−1, as previously reported [29,30].

The presence of S, N, O and C atoms for S,N-GQDs was indicated in the XPS survey spectrum (Fig. S3a), being the atomic concentrations of 8%, 5.8%, 34.1% and 56.3% respectively, as calculated by EDX. Deconvolution of the high-resolution spectrum XPS for C 1s (Fig. S3b) gives three peaks at 284.5, 285 and 288.2 eV attributed to three different environments of carbon, starting from an sp2-hybridized graphitic C=C, an sp3 carbon from C–S, C–N and C–O bonds, and the C=O from carbonyl and carboxyl functional groups, respectively. Similarly, two peaks for N 1s at 399.6 and 401.2 eV were obtained and ascribed to the pyridinic (C–N–C) and pyrrolic (N–(C)3) types, as depicted in Fig. S3c. The high-resolution O 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. S3d) shows three peaks at 530.9, 532.1 and 534.9 eV attributed to oxygen KLL auger, C=O and C–O species, respectively [31,32]. Fig. S3e depicts the S 2p curve which contains two kind of bonding environments for sulfur in C-S-C bonding pattern, the peaks at 162.9 and 164.3 eV associated to the S 2p3 /2 and the S 2p½, respectively.

NC prepared from different methods [13,25] were evaluated for the gelation process but only those obtained with TEMPO-radical is able to form hydrogels. TEMPO-oxidized NC resulted to have a high degree of surface hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, as described in the IR data (Fig. 1b) as well as a large specific surface area. The hierarchical structure of these nanofibers containing negative charges can be self-associated in presence of salts. The gel-like behavior of NC is due to the entanglements between nanofibrils in aqueous media via hydrogen bonding (Fig. 2 a), which gelation is reversible and can become a fluid again when stirred at high shear rate or when heated. This hydrogel could be dissolved by high shearing and reformed more than 10 times without any difficulty because of the non-covalent interactions involved. A visual examination of the gelation formation was achieved using the “inversion test” (Fig. 3 a).

As implied in the reported literature, it is important to evaluate the proportions of NC and the fluorophore for the gel formation (Fig. 2b). Regarding to the gelator, a high concentration of NC hinders the gelation process in a homogeneous way by virtue of NP aggregation, whereas low NC content is not able to strongly form a stable 3D system for the low degree of hydrogen bonding. In the same way, the proportions of the GQDs embedded into the NC hydrogels were studied, in one hand, for preventing destabilization of the 3D-gel matrix and, in the other hand, for enhancing the optical properties of GQDs as a result of the lack of NP aggregation.

The ideal amount of NC required for the development of stable hydrogels containing S,N-GQDs is ranging from 45 to 55 mg for 500 μL of ultrapure water [26]. For the gelation experiments, S,N-GQDs at different concentrations were mixed with NC (Fig. S4). On the one hand, inversion tests of the hydrogels revealed that 35 and 40 mg of NC were not enough for the hydrogel formation whereas 45 mg provided good consistency. On the other hand, amounts above 55 mg resulted in a very viscous and dense hydrogel with a heterogeneous distribution of nanomaterials. Therefore, 45 mg were chosen as optimal quantity of NC (9%wt) for hydrogel formation when using the optimized S,N-GQD concentration (Fig. 3a), which appearance in fibrillated networks (Fig. 3b) is in contrast to those platelets characteristic of organogels based on same NC type [11]. Interestingly, the transition to hydrogels composed of NC in presence of S,N-GQDs occurred only at pH values above 6, in contrast to NC hydrogels which were stable even at the low pH value of 3 [33]. The GQDs/NC hydrogels at pH 6 were stable upon standing for a few months (up to 6 months) at room temperature or under refrigeration.

Interestingly, although the significant increase on the fluorescence intensity of GQD when immersed into NC hydrogels, excitation and emission wavelengths of the hydrogel remain the same compared to the GQD aqueous solutions, as depicted in Fig. S5. This can be an indicative of the lack of aggregation of the graphene monolayer via π- π stacking interaction, which would be resulted in a reduction of fluorescence intensity. It can be stated that the oxygen-containing NC can electrostatically interact with the superficial groups of graphene layers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Behavior of the fluorescent hydrogel towards laccase enzyme
ce:section.ce:para: A convenient route for detecting laccase is based on the fluorescence quenching of S,N-GQDs. With the aim of figuring out the roles of the hydrogel components in presence of laccase, the interaction of the enzyme with the individual nanoparticles (NC and S,N-GQDs) in different media (aqueous solutions and hydrogels) were explored.

On the one hand, the enzyme laccase affected exclusively the fluorescence intensity of the GQD sheets in both aqueous solution and hydrogel in different ways, accordingly to preliminary studies. It is important to emphasize the importance of preparing hydrogels in which the nanoparticles and the analyte are well distributed through the 3D-network; thus, the enzyme was immersed into the hydrogel sensing system as follows: 250 μL of laccase (at a concentration level of 33 U mL−1) was added into well-dispersed aqueous solutions (250 μL) containing S,N-GQDs at different concentrations (0.8–4 mg mL−1). After 5 min of incubation, 9%wt NC was added to achieved the formation of gels; thus, fluorescence monitoring was performed and evaluated. The results are depicted in Fig. S4, in which the maximized interaction between the analyte and the sensing platform (laccase- GQDs/NC) occurs at a final GQD concentration of 2 mg mL−1. Results confirm the well dispersion of the graphitic nanodots at this concentration into the NC matrix for achieving the maximum fluorescence variation in presence of laccase, in accordance to Fig. S1. It is worthy to mention that physical adsorption may take place possibly via electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions as occurred for most of protein immobilizations onto carbon-based nanoparticles [34], thus, discarding the covalent linking of the enzyme to the graphene surface as a result of both unaltered emission and excitation wavelengths.

On the other hand, notably, enzyme immobilized into GQDs/NC hydrogels exhibited significant signal stability if compared to those GQD solutions as sensor platform. Fig. S6 shows the evolution of the fluorescence signal of the S,N-GQDs over time in aqueous and hydrogel media. In case of the sensing aqueous solutions, a stable signal was achieved after 20 min whereas in the hydrogels only 5 min was required. It should be noted that GQD-enzyme interaction is favored in the hydrogel matrix via non-covalent forces. Thus, an energy transfer process between the S,N-GQDs and laccase is believed to be causing this quenching of fluorescence in the GQDs/NC hydrogel. These results indicated that NC 3D-networks are ideal supports for enzyme stabilization and immobilization.

In order to determine the stability of the resulting fluorescent hydrogels, the melting temperature (T
gel) of the gel was calculated; thus, a metallic sphere (weight of 0.5 g and diameter of 5 mm) was placed onto the surface of the hydrogel using a heating range from 30 °C to 72 °C at a heating rate of 4 °C min−1. The GQDs/NC hydrogel was found to be melted at 70 °C whereas the Laccase-immobilized GQDs/NC hydrogel was found to be at 66 °C.

We envisioned that the proposed nanohybrid hydrogel could be used as stabilizing matrix to trap the enzyme from a complex matrix and to reuse it after stored protected from environmental extreme conditions. Thus, the enzyme could be stored in the nanocellulosic hydrogel and recovered from it for further uses.

Before evaluating the capability of detecting laccase, emphasis is laid on the effect of different substances like (bio)molecules or metal ions onto the fluorescence probe. Preliminary experiments demonstrated that the fluorescence intensity of the S,N-GQDs/NC hydrogel was not significantly affected by the presence of metal ions (Ag(I), Na(I), Ca(II), Mg(II), Co(II), Zn(II), Fe(III)), organic molecules (citric and salicylic acids) either the acetylcholinesterase enzyme, as depicted in Fig. S7. Only the combination of all metal ions resulted in a diminution of 7.6% in the fluorescence intensity of the sensing probe.

Afterwards, the fluorescence responses of the resulting hydrogel upon increasing concentrations of laccase were investigated under the optimal conditions. The experiments resulted in a reduction of the fluorescence intensity of the hydrogel by increasing the laccase concentration. Thus, these optical changes were used for laccase detection. As aforementioned, the role of NC gel was to achieve faster a stable fluorescence emission signal of S,N-GQDs through the stabilization of the interaction between analyte and graphitic nanostructure.

The proposed methodology was investigated in terms of analytical efficiency, in order to evaluate its usefulness for quantitative analysis of laccase. Fig. 4 a shows the variation of fluorescence curves of the hydrogel at different laccase concentrations. Fig. 4b depicts the linear behavior of the GQDs/NC hydrogel fluorescence versus laccase concentration in the range of 0.01–10 U mL−1, calculated by the Stern-Volmer equation. Each concentration level of the calibration graph was analyzed in triplicate.

The method detection limit (MDL) was calculated according to the code of federal regulation [35] at an enzyme concentration of 0.05 U mL−1. The MDL was calculated as standard deviation of 5 replicates multiplied by Student value (t) at the 99% confident level with 4 degree of freedom. The MDL obtained for Laccase was 0.048 U mL−1. The method quantification limit (MQL) was calculated as three times the MDL being 0.146 U mL−1.
The precision, in terms of repeatability (n = 5), expressed as RSD were of 1.36% and 1.14% at concentration levels of 0.15 U mL−1 and 1 U mL−1, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of laccase in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing hydrogel was lastly validated in color protect shampoo samples which were purchased from a local supermarket (Córdoba, Spain). The determination of laccase was not directly performed due to the complexity of the sample matrix, which was a gel-like fluid. Since the method is proposed for aqueous solution samples, it was needed to perform a preliminary LLE and SPE steps to extract the laccase to an aqueous solution removing any type of solid or other possible soluble interference.

Afterwards, the treated shampoos were analyzed using the proposed methodology. Two different commercial shampoos were selected: L'Oreal Elvive Color Vive and Schwarzkoft Color Protect. Relative recoveries were calculated as the percentage of the ratio between the concentration of laccase found in the spiked shampoo samples after performing the extraction protocol and the initial spiked concentration. Acceptable values of recovery were obtained in the range of 86.2–94.1% for both samples at two different concentration levels, namely, 0.15 U mL−1 and 5 U mL−1. The reproducibility of the sensing hydrogel system in shampoo samples were calculated in quintuplicate spanning the range from 1.83 to 3.33%. Table 1 summarizes the recoveries and RSD obtained for each shampoo sample.

The figures of merit of this method are satisfactory in a wide range of concentrations if compared to other methodologies involving the monitorization of the enzymatic activity by resonance scattering, voltammetry and spectrophotometric and spectrofluorimetric techniques (Table S1).

Results evidence the preparation of a responsive hydrogel as an elegant approach for sensing applications. This NP-NP self-assembly technology platform is capable of detecting and storage the laccase enzyme for a period. This strategy is expected to open new opportunities in designing novel NP-based ultrasensitive detection and imaging systems, especially for being all components biodegradables with low cost production.

We have demonstrated a new method of sensing laccase in a complex matrix by fluorescence quenching of S,N-GQDs immersed into a NC hydrogel without measuring their catalytic activity as most of reports, taking into account that the enzymatic activity is strongly affected by the presence of inhibitors, inducers and other environmental conditions such as pH, ionic strength and even temperature. The fluorescent sensing hydrogel is simple, eco-friendly and easily prepared from biocompatible nanoparticles which self-organized via electrostatic interactions. GQDs/NC hydrogels are firstly used as a novel alternative for enzyme immobilization and laccase monitoring. Thus, it is expected that laccase weakly interacted with the graphitic layers stabilized by NC leading to the energy transfer, and consequently to the fluorescence quenching of the GQDs immersed into the NC hydrogels. Nevertheless, cosmetics are very consistent and complex samples; thus, a suitable method involving a LLE and SPE for fluorescent determination of laccase in shampoos was applied. The analytical evaluation revealed good sensitivity and specificity of the method towards laccase.

This work opens up new avenues for the great interest aroused by the self-assembly of environmental-sensitive biomolecules in hydrogels, as a promising approach to create cost-effective multifunctional materials, especially those containing immobilized enzymes for their catalytic activity [36]. Furthermore, the possibility of creating enzyme-immobilized nanogel have a great potential in bioremediation, in selective areas of textile sector [18,37], and in biomedicine as tissue bioscaffolds for therapeutic release [38] and healing prosthesis [39].

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to express their gratitude to the Spanish Ministry of Innovation and Science for funding the Project CTQ2014-52939R.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Online data Online data

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2017.04.018.

NC, nanocellulose; S,N-GQDs, sulfur, nitrogen-codoped graphene quantum dots; GQDs, graphene quantum dots; NP, nanoparticle; LLE, liquid-liquid extraction; SPE, solid phase extraction; RSD, relative standard deviation; MDL, method detection limit; MQL, method quantification limit; IR, infrared spectroscopy; ATR, attenuated total reflectance; EDX, energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy; SEM, scanning electron microscopy; Tgel, gel melting temperature; HR-TEM, high resolution transmission electron microscopy; XPS, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy; Φ; quantum yield.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent progress in strategies for the creation of protein-based fluorescent biosensors
The rational design of a peptide-based hydrogel responsive to H2S
Fluorescent carbon quantum dot hydrogels for direct determination of silver ions
Composites of polymer hydrogles and nanoparticulate systems for biomedical and pharmaceutical applications
Aerogels from CdSe/CdS nanorods with ultra-long exciton lifetimes and high fluorescence quantum yields
“Smart” materials based on cellulose: a review of the preparations, properties, and applications
Filler-nanocellulose substrate for printed electronics: experiments and model approach to structure and conductivity
ß-cyclodextrin decorated nanocellulose: a smart approach towards the selective fluorimetric determination of danofloxacin in milk samples
